structure.description: Renders three independent navigation rails over the same primary entity: hierarchical (folder tree), relational (link/backlink graph), classifier (tag tree). Each rail filters/scopes the same catalog body without shadowing the others — user picks the lens, not the data.

rationale.hypothesis: Knowledge bases have non-tree semantics: any one taxonomy is lossy. Forcing a single nav axis (Notion's page tree, Confluence's space tree) hides the long-tail of cross-cutting access. Three orthogonal rails preserve all three intuitions without forcing the author to canonicalize prematurely.

evidence.description: Left sidebar has Files / Tags / Bookmarks tabs — switchable rails over the same Note
Pages / Tags / Graph each act as a top-level entry into the same atom
Sidebar surfaces page-list AND tag-list AND linked-references separately

counterexample.description: Page-as-database forces single-tree; relations are second-class — the orthogonal-rail pattern is a deliberate departure
Single project hierarchy works fine when the entity is small and process-driven; orthogonal rails would be over-engineering

shouldMatch.reason: Page has folder-hierarchy (parentPageId), wiki-link edges (Block targetPageId), and tag-like properties — three rails fit
